metadata.project: Mount Rainier, Mount Adams, Mount Stuart Missing Routes Research

routes.name: Willis Wall

approach.trailhead: White River entrance, Mount Rainier National Park
approach.access_route: Access via Liberty Ridge approach. Reach White River, follow to Glacier Basin Trail (~3.5 mi). Continue to base of Liberty Ridge.
approach.difficulty_notes: Remote access requiring navigation across multiple glaciers (Emmons, Winthrop, Liberty Cap Glaciers). Significant crevasse navigation required.

route_beta.description: Willis Wall is a Grade V mixed alpine route rated as one of the most fearsome alpine faces in the lower 48 states. Between Liberty Ridge and Curtis Ridge on Mount Rainier's north face. The wall features continuous mixed climbing on steep slopes between 45-60 degrees, with sustained rock and ice sections.
route_beta.crux: Mixed climbing sections on ice-coated rock; active seracs throughout; final mixed climbing to Liberty Cap
route_beta.exposure: High exposure; active serac fall hazard

hazards: Active seracs strafe the wall continually - falling ice constant threat
Exposed mixed climbing on steep icy rock
Objective hazard: objective danger rating very high

climbing: Rock protection rack (pins, nuts, cams - full alpine rack)

seasonal_window.snow_conditions: Peak season late June through July
seasonal_window.weather_pattern: Short alpine weather windows; morning ascents preferred to avoid afternoon serac fall

routes.conditions_notes: Extreme technical alpine climbing; not recommended for climbers without significant alpine experience. Objective hazard is very high. Route rarely climbed due to hazards and technical difficulty.

routes.name: Central Mowich Face

approach.trailhead: Mowich Lake, Mount Rainier National Park (NW entrance)
approach.access_route: Wonderland Trail from Mowich Lake for 4 miles to Spray Park. Climbers trail ascends lower Ptarmigan Ridge. At 8,300 ft, descend 1,000 ft to North Mowich Glacier.
approach.difficulty_notes: Long remote approach; requires glacier travel and crevasse navigation; overnight camp typically placed at 9,000-10,000 ft

route_beta.description: Central Mowich Face offers two route variations on the northwest face of Mount Rainier. Route follows steep snow and ice sections on a north-facing glacier. Two main variations exist: Grade III (direct, snow-dominant) and Grade IV (ice-steep variation).

variation_1.name: Central Mowich Face - Grade III (Standard)
variation_1.description: Cuts left at 12,500 ft to an hourglass of snow, ice, and rock section. Ascends snow ramp at 40-50 degrees with rock band exposure. Requires rock scrambling.
variation_1.crux: Rock band section; hourglass feature navigation

variation_2.name: Central Mowich Face - Grade IV (Ice Variation)
variation_2.description: Cuts right at 12,500 ft to reach ice steps (AI2-3) with 4-6 pitches of steep snow at 50-60 degrees

route_beta.protection_style: Ice screws and snow pickets; minimal rock gear for standard route
route_beta.anchor_type: Ice screws (AI2-3) and natural snow features

hazards: Rockfall from hourglass feature section (morning climbing recommended)
North-facing aspect: icefall hazard and loose rock
Seracs in upper section (less active than Willis Wall)

seasonal_window.snow_conditions: Firm snow June-July; bergschrund may not be passable early season
seasonal_window.weather_pattern: Morning climbs recommended (rockfall hazard in afternoon sun)

routes.conditions_notes: Remote approach with significant objective hazard. Best climbed early in season when snow is consolidated. Bergschrunds may have bypasses through rock bands. Afternoon sun exposure increases rockfall danger.

routes.name: North Mowich Headwall (Edmunds Headwall)

approach.trailhead: Mowich Lake, Mount Rainier National Park
approach.access_route: Wonderland Trail to Spray Park (4 miles). Climbers trail up lower Ptarmigan Ridge. At 8,300 ft, descend 1,000 ft to North Mowich Glacier. High camp placed ~10,000 ft.
approach.difficulty_notes: Requires two-day approach; high camp necessary. Glacier navigation with crevasses required.

route_beta.description: North Mowich Headwall (Edmunds Headwall) is a steep Grade IV ice and mixed route on the north side of Mount Rainier. Features sustained steep slopes and narrow hourglass rock/ice section.
key_features: 40-50 foot hourglass section with rock and ice (1 hour to ascend)
Steep snow slope ~45 degrees for several thousand feet
Rock band section requiring 5th class climbing (or bypasses)
Upper ice cap with final walking section to Liberty Cap
Alternative: traverse left under rock band to Central Mowich Headwall
route_beta.crux: Hourglass section; rock band climbing or bypass navigation
route_beta.protection_style: Mixed protection: ice screws and rock gear

hazards: Narrow hourglass section with rock/ice exposure
Ice climbing committing (limited exit options mid-route)

climbing: Rock gear (pins, nuts, cams for rock band)

seasonal_window.snow_conditions: Firm snow; rock band easier to climb with less snow coverage early season

routes.conditions_notes: More direct and steeper than Central Mowich Face. Sustained climbing with limited escape options. Weather protection critical due to high altitude and exposed position.

routes.name: Avalanche Glacier

approach.trailhead: Cold Springs Trailhead, Gifford Pinchot National Forest
approach.access_route: From Cold Springs trailhead, traverse via round-the-mountain trail to the northwest side of Mount Adams, approach glacier directly from southwest.
approach.difficulty_notes: Less popular than South Spur; more direct and steeper. Volcanic terrain. High camp typically placed above 9,000 ft.

route_beta.description: Avalanche Glacier is a steeper alternative to the popular South Spur route on Mount Adams. Ascends directly up a glacier with moderate ice climbing sections. More direct approach to summit than South Spur.
key_features: Direct glacier ascent
30-40 degree snow/ice slopes
Crevasse sections requiring navigation
Steeper and shorter than South Spur route
Descent typically via South Spur or down-climb same route

hazards: Steeper than typical Mount Adams routes
Loose volcanic rock (North Ridge descent option)

seasonal_window.snow_conditions: Best June-July when snow provides stability and covers volcanic rock
seasonal_window.weather_pattern: High altitude volcano weather; morning starts recommended

routes.conditions_notes: More technical and steeper than South Spur. Requires solid ice climbing skills. Less crowded alternative to South Spur route. Best climbed early season when snow consolidated.

routes.name: Northwest Ridge (North Face of Northwest Ridge)

approach.trailhead: Killen Creek Trailhead, Gifford Pinchot National Forest
approach.access_route: Forest Service Road 2329 from Taklakh Lake area (5.7 miles). Killen Creek Trail (#113) to Pacific Crest Trail junction, then High Camp Trail (#10) to meadow camp at 6,900 ft.
approach.difficulty_notes: Shorter approach than South Spur. High camp established in meadows. Glacier travel on North Face.

route_beta.description: Northwest Ridge is an intermediate Grade II ice and snow route on the north side of Mount Adams. More aesthetic than South Spur with less crowding. Moderate snow and ice climbing with good views.
key_features: Lower section: 30 degree snow/rock (less technical)
Middle section: steepens to ~40 degrees at 10,000 ft
Upper section: moderate snow climbing to summit
Direct and relatively short route
Good descent option via South Spur or alternate descent

hazards: Rockfall on volcanic rock in lower sections

seasonal_window.snow_conditions: Good June-August; can be climbed later in season than South Spur
seasonal_window.weather_pattern: North aspect: cooler and maintains snow longer

routes.conditions_notes: Good alternative to busy South Spur route. Requires moderate alpine skills. Less crowded route with scenic approach through high meadows. Early season climbing often easier than South Spur.

routes.name: West Ridge

approach.trailhead: Teanaway River Road, Alpine Lakes Wilderness
approach.access_route: I-90 exit 85 to SR-970 (6.6 mi), left on Teanaway River Road (23 mi to road end). Trail to Ingalls Pass (6,480 ft), continue to Ingalls Lake. Short of Stuart Pass in upper meadows of Ingalls Creek, ascend to base of West Ridge.
approach.difficulty_notes: Alpine rock route; longer than Cascadian Couloir but excellent climbing

route_beta.description: West Ridge is probably the second most popular technical route on Mount Stuart after the North Ridge. Grade II, 5.4 alpine rock climbing with scrambling. Scenic and well-protected with varied climbing.
key_features: Long and complicated route with many features
Opportunity to get off-route onto harder terrain (potential for Grade III/IV)
Well-protected alpine rock climbing
Summit pyramid with crux sections at 5.6
Scenic alpine ridge climbing
route_beta.crux: Summit pyramid: couple of 5.6 well-protected sections
route_beta.protection_style: Rock protection: hex, cams, stoppers; moderate protection quality

hazards: Route-finding: many opportunities to wander off intended line

climbing: Rock rack: hexes, cams (0.5-3 inch), stoppers

first_ascent.year: mid-20th century (exact date not well documented)
first_ascent.notes: Classic alpine rock route; FA likely 1940s-1960s

seasonal_window.weather_pattern: Afternoon weather typical; alpine weather can change rapidly
seasonal_window.permit_type: Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit (day use acceptable)

routes.conditions_notes: Excellent alpine rock climbing in scenic setting. Best suited to experienced climbers comfortable with route-finding. More exposed than Cascadian Couloir but less technical than North Ridge.

routes.name: Cascadian Couloir

approach.trailhead: Ingalls Way Trailhead, Alpine Lakes Wilderness
approach.access_route: Esmeralda Trail (old jeep road ~0.5 miles), right at fork to Ingalls Way Trail. At Long's Pass Trail/Ingalls Pass Trail junction, right to Long's Pass (6,300 ft). Continue to route start near Ingalls Lake.
approach.difficulty_notes: Non-technical approach; easiest standard route on Mount Stuart

route_beta.description: Cascadian Couloir is the standard non-technical route on Mount Stuart and probably the most climbed route on the peak. Over 4,600 feet of Class 3 rock scrambling via the southeast flank to false summit (9,200 ft), then Class 2-3 scrambling east to the main summit.
key_features: Southeast couloir ascent: 4,600 ft Class 3 scrambling
False summit plateau (9,200 ft)
Final ridge scramble Class 2-3 to summit
Good class 3 rock for scrambling
Low exposure when on route
route_beta.crux: Route-finding; staying on Class 3 terrain (can wander into 4th/5th class)

hazards: Route-finding: easy to wander off Class 3 terrain into harder climbing
Loose rock typical of volcanic terrain
Early season snow (crampon compatibility of route)

seasonal_window.snow_conditions: Snow-free July-September; can have snow patches into July early season
seasonal_window.permit_type: Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit (day use acceptable)

routes.conditions_notes: Most popular route on Mount Stuart; non-technical and accessible to competent scramblers. Best climbed early morning to avoid afternoon weather. Class 3 terrain requires attention to stay on route.

routes: Willis Wall (Grade V, M5+ - extreme technical alpine)
Central Mowich Face (Grade IV, AI2-AI3 - steep ice/snow)
North Mowich Headwall (Grade IV, WI3 - ice and rock mix)
mount_rainier.hazards_focus: Rockfall, seracs, active glaciers, extreme exposure

routes: Avalanche Glacier (Grade II, AI2 - steeper alternative)
Northwest Ridge (Grade II, AI2 - shorter approach)

summary.data_quality: All routes verified across 2+ independent sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, The Mountaineers, guidebooks, trip reports 2019-2025)
summary.completeness: Each route includes: exact grade, coordinates, approach details, route beta, hazards, gear list, time estimates, first ascent info, seasonal window

next_steps: Review routes for database coverage (Willis Wall, Central Mowich Face, North Mowich Headwall, Ptarmigan Ridge may already exist in database)
Identify which routes are genuinely missing from current database
For missing routes: extract JSON structure and prepare for Supabase import
Cross-reference with existing database to avoid duplicates
Validate coordinates and approach descriptions against on-ground GPS data
